manifest: Fractera agents do not deliver an automation in final form straight from a request — they build a platform for developing repeatable automations: standardized reuse, a visual interface, input/output data in the local DB and vector memory, fast switching from the user UI. Fractera lets Hermes and a coding agent build a finished-cycle tool — an n8n for one single task: the user does not recreate the task, they open it in the UI, run it and track the result.
about: Starter interface of ONE project of the Projects layer: a description block (why / what is automated / how it works), an interactive react-flow process diagram driven by declarative data, a cron-queue table and a results table (both empty until the cron infrastructure fills them). Mounted OUTSIDE [lang] into the monolingual, architect+manager-gated projects zone — access and language are INHERITED from the zone layout (app/(projects)/layout.tsx), so this primitive carries NO role/i18n seams. Composed by file copy + token substitution — no code generation.

envelope.zone: app/(projects)/projects — the category folder must already exist in the slot
envelope.rendering: dynamic (the zone role gate reads cookies — the sanctioned cockpit exception to static-first)
envelope.roles: architect+manager, inherited from the zone layout — not injectable, not overridable
envelope.i18n: mono (the site's default language, inherited from the zone layout)

seams.diagram-as-data: The react-flow diagram is rendered by one fixed client component; its shape lives in _data/flow.ts (plain nodes/edges + per-node info). Nodes are movable (edges follow) and every node opens an info panel — its summary, processes and connections — but the canvas can never create or delete nodes/edges. Finishing the diagram for the real project = editing that DATA — a coding-agent handoff, never a template change.
seams.tables-p6: _lib/project-data.ts reads project_cron_runs from the shared app DB (filtered by this category/project) — rows are written by the substrate cron runner (fractera-cron). Declaring jobs for the project = a cron.json next to the page; the runner picks it up within a tick, no restart.
seams.workflow-as-execution: app/api/projects/<category>/<project>/_workflow/definition.ts is the durable Workflow DevKit function — it lives next to its run route under app/api/, NOT in the page folder (WDK derives the workflow name from the file path and forbids the parentheses of route groups like (projects)) — the EXECUTABLE mirror of the diagram data (steps work/store/publish correspond to the flow nodes; the trigger node is the run route). fractera-cron stays the ONLY scheduler: a cron.json http action posts to /api/projects/<category>/<project>/run, which start()s the workflow; WDK's own scheduling is not used. The workflow journals its run into project_cron_runs (created_by='wdk') through the slot's db layer, so runs appear in the page tables with no page changes. Finishing the workflow for the real project = editing the step bodies in _workflow/definition.ts — a coding-agent handoff, never a template change.
seams.declaration: README.md carries the /service/architecture machine block (fractera:meta) with visibility/roles + cron/integrations, so the composed project round-trips through the declare panel.

params.category: Existing projects category slug (automation | fractera-pages | personal | other). Fixed English identifiers.
params.project: Project slug, kebab-case English identifier — the folder name IS the registry (never localized, never renamed casually).
params.title: Human project name shown on the page.
params.purpose: Why the project exists (description block). Optional — placeholder text if omitted.
params.automation: What is automated. Optional — placeholder text if omitted.
params.how: How the automation works. Optional — placeholder text if omitted.
params.cron: true/false — the project will run cron processes. Recorded in the README declaration only; execution is the P6 cron infrastructure.
params.integrations: JSON array [{ name, envKeys[] }] — external integrations with their env keys. Recorded in the README declaration; keys are materialized into app/.env.local via the env setter + rebuild when executing.
